seoTitle: Delete a Transaction in QuickBooks Online Safely
metaDescription: Learn how to find and delete an unwanted transaction in QuickBooks Online, when to void instead, and how to check the Audit Log afterward.
intro: Duplicate entries or mistaken transactions can throw off your reconciliations if left uncorrected. This guide shows how to locate and delete a transaction in QuickBooks Online, when voiding is the safer choice, and how to confirm the removal in your Audit Log.

steps.heading: Locate the transaction
steps.body: Go to Transactions, then Chart of Accounts, select the relevant register, and click View Register, or use the Search tool to filter by transaction type.

steps.heading: Open the transaction form
steps.body: Click the row to expand its details, then click Edit to open the original entry.

steps.heading: Choose delete or void
steps.body: In the More options drawer at the bottom, decide whether to Delete the entry entirely or use Void to preserve the record while zeroing it out.

steps.heading: Confirm the action
steps.body: Click Yes on the confirmation prompt to let QuickBooks recalculate your account balances.

steps.heading: Check the Audit Log
steps.body: Open the Audit Log from the gear icon to verify the deleted or voided transaction is indexed for compliance.

faq.question: What's the difference between deleting and voiding a transaction?
faq.answer: Deleting removes the transaction entirely, while voiding keeps a record of it but zeroes out its financial impact, which is often safer for reconciled entries.

faq.question: Can I recover a deleted transaction?
faq.answer: You can find its details in the Audit Log and manually re-enter it, but the original transaction itself cannot be restored automatically.

faq.question: Will deleting a transaction affect my reconciliation?
faq.answer: Yes, deleting a previously reconciled transaction can throw off your reconciled balance, so review carefully before deleting.

faq.question: How do I confirm a transaction was deleted?
faq.answer: Check the Audit Log, which tracks all deleted and voided transactions along with who made the change.
